status_notes: Initial research from AWS WAF and Route 53 documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live AWS environment).
notes: Web application firewall and DNS. WAF protects CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync. Route 53 provides DNS with DNSSEC.

match_nodes.description: AWS and Marketplace managed rule groups

data.description: Pre-built rule groups maintained by AWS and AWS Marketplace sellers. AWS managed rules cover common threats. Rule groups include Core Rule Set (CRS), SQL injection, XSS, known bad inputs, anonymous IP list, bot control, and account takeover prevention.

options.description: Core protection against common web exploits. Includes OWASP Top 10 coverage.
SQL injection attack patterns.
Request patterns known to be malicious (Log4j, path traversal, etc.).
IPs from VPNs, proxies, Tor exit nodes, hosting providers.
Bot detection and management. Common (basic bots) and Targeted (sophisticated bots) levels.
Account Takeover Prevention. Detects credential stuffing and brute force.
Account Creation Fraud Prevention. Detects fake account creation.

data.vendor_recommendation: Start with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet and AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet as a baseline. Add SQLi and XSS rule sets for applications with form inputs. Use Bot Control for APIs and applications with bot concerns.

audit.note: Check Rules array for ManagedRuleGroupStatement entries. Scope is REGIONAL for ALB/API Gateway, CLOUDFRONT for CloudFront.

configure.cli: aws wafv2 create-web-acl --name {name} --scope REGIONAL --default-action Allow={} --rules file://rules.json

data.description: User-defined rules that match on request attributes. Match conditions include IP address, geographic location, request size, regex patterns, SQL injection detection, XSS detection, and label matching. Actions include Allow, Block, Count, CAPTCHA, and Challenge.

options.description: Match against named IP sets
Match by country code
Match by request component size
Match by regex patterns
Built-in SQL injection detection on specified fields
Built-in XSS detection on specified fields
Match labels added by other rules for complex logic
Allow the request
Block the request with configurable response code
Count but do not block (for testing)
Require CAPTCHA completion
Require silent browser challenge (JavaScript)

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Count action when testing new rules before switching to Block. Use labels for complex multi-rule logic. Combine geo-match with other conditions for nuanced policies.

audit.note: Check Rules array for Rule entries (non-managed). Review Statement and Action fields.

data.description: Automatically rate-limit requests based on configurable thresholds. Tracks request count per IP address (or other key) over a 5-minute evaluation window. When threshold is exceeded, the source is blocked until the request rate drops below the threshold.

options.description: Maximum requests per 5-minute window per aggregation key
How to group requests for counting
Optional additional match conditions to narrow which requests are counted

data.vendor_recommendation: Use rate-based rules as a first line of defense against DDoS and brute-force attacks. Set thresholds based on expected legitimate traffic patterns. Use scope-down statements to protect specific endpoints (login pages, APIs).

audit.note: Check for rules with RateBasedStatement in the Statement field.

match_nodes.description: Named lists of IPs for allow/deny

data.description: Named, reusable collections of IP addresses and CIDR ranges. IP sets can be referenced in multiple rules across multiple web ACLs. Support IPv4 and IPv6. Maximum 10,000 addresses per IP set.

options.description: List of IPs or CIDR ranges. Max 10,000 per set.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use IP sets for frequently updated lists (known malicious IPs, corporate allowlists). Centralize IP management in IP sets rather than hardcoding in rules. Use AWS Firewall Manager to share IP sets across accounts.

data.description: Log all requests inspected by the web ACL, including match details, rule actions, and request metadata. Logging destinations include S3, CloudWatch Logs, and Kinesis Data Firehose. Essential for security monitoring, incident investigation, and rule tuning.

options.description: Cost-effective long-term storage. Bucket name must start with aws-waf-logs-.
Real-time monitoring and alerting. Log group name must start with aws-waf-logs-.
Real-time streaming to analytics services. Delivery stream name must start with aws-waf-logs-.

default.note: WAF logging is not enabled by default. Must be explicitly configured.

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable logging on all web ACLs. Use S3 for long-term storage and CloudWatch Logs for real-time alerting. Configure log filters to reduce volume if needed (e.g., log only blocked requests). Analyze logs regularly to tune rules.

audit.note: Error response indicates logging is not configured.
audit.console: AWS WAF → Web ACLs → {ACL} → Logging and metrics

match_nodes.description: AWS managed bot detection and management

data.description: AWS Bot Control managed rule group for detecting and managing bot traffic. Two protection levels: Common (detects self-identifying bots, basic scraping) and Targeted (detects sophisticated bots that attempt to evade detection). Provides bot labels for custom rule logic.

options.description: Detects common bots using request patterns. Included in WAF base pricing.
ML-based detection of sophisticated bots. Additional per-request charges. Detects credential stuffing, scraping bots that mimic browsers.

data.vendor_recommendation: Start with Common bot control to identify bot traffic. Use bot labels in custom rules for nuanced handling (e.g., allow verified search engine bots, challenge unverified bots, block known bad bots). Add Targeted level for high-value applications.

audit.note: Check for ManagedRuleGroupStatement with VendorName=AWS and Name=AWSManagedRulesBotControlRuleSet.
audit.console: AWS WAF → Web ACLs → {ACL} → Rules → Bot Control

match_nodes.description: DNSSEC signing for Route 53 hosted zones

data.description: DNSSEC signing for Route 53 public hosted zones. Protects against DNS spoofing and cache poisoning by cryptographically signing DNS records. Route 53 manages KSK (Key Signing Key) in AWS KMS and automatically manages ZSK (Zone Signing Key) rotation.

options.description: Customer-managed key in KMS. Used to sign the DNSKEY record set.
AWS-managed. Used to sign all other record sets. Auto-rotated.
Must be added to parent zone (domain registrar) to complete the chain of trust.

default.note: DNSSEC signing is not enabled by default on hosted zones.

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable DNSSEC for all public hosted zones, especially those serving critical applications. Ensure the DS record is properly configured at the domain registrar. Monitor DNSSEC status with Route 53 health checks.

audit.note: Check Status.ServeSignature field. SIGNING means DNSSEC is active.

match_nodes.description: Monitor endpoint health and DNS failover

data.description: Route 53 health checks monitor the health of endpoints and trigger DNS failover. Health checks can monitor HTTP/HTTPS endpoints, TCP connections, CloudWatch alarms, or calculated status from other health checks. Used with failover routing for high availability.

options.description: Check endpoint availability. Configurable path, port, search string, request interval.
TCP connection check to endpoint.
Combine multiple health checks with AND/OR logic.
Health status based on CloudWatch alarm state.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use health checks with failover routing for critical services. Configure health checks to check application health (not just connectivity) by verifying response body content. Set appropriate thresholds for failure detection.
